title: Metallic powder in carrageenan marbling
subtitle: Mixing mica powder into acrylic pigment to add gold or silver veins to a marbled pattern
excerpt: Mica powder mixed into a small amount of extended acrylic pigment creates a metallic colour that floats on carrageenan size alongside standard pigments. Applied last as an accent drop, it adds gold or silver veins to a stone pattern or Spanish wave.
sourceNotes: Josef Halfer, The Progress of the Marbling Art (1893, English translation by William F. Goss, 1894): public domain. Halfer notes the use of gold leaf on size in Turkish and Persian marbling traditions. Mica powder is a modern equivalent that produces a similar metallic effect without the handling difficulty of loose gold leaf.

glossaryTerms.definition: Finely ground mica mineral in gold, silver, copper, or bronze, used to add a metallic sheen to marbled patterns. Mixed with acrylic pigment and extra ox-gall; applied as a drop on top of base colours already laid on the size.

A marbling pattern produced by dropping colours randomly on the size without any combing. The result resembles the veined surface of agate or granite. One of the simplest marbling patterns; well suited to metallic accent drops.
A marbling pattern produced by drawing a wide-tooth comb at an angle through a laid pattern. The oblique comb direction produces a flowing, wave-like motion in the colour bands.

content.text: Traditional Turkish and Persian marbling sometimes used gold leaf pressed onto the size surface to add a metallic accent among the painted veins.

is the practical modern equivalent. Ground mica suspended in acrylic pigment floats on carrageenan size just like standard pigments. The metallic colour does not comb as cleanly, so it works best as an accent on unstructured patterns:
rather than precise French combed grids. Dropped last over the base colours, it creates gold or silver veins running through the coloured background.

attrs.title: Marbling chemistry and mica dust
attrs.body: Alum used for mordanting paper is a mild skin and eye irritant. Wear nitrile gloves when mixing or applying it. Measuring mica powder from the bag produces a fine dust: wear a dust mask during this step and measure over a piece of paper to contain the spillage. Acrylic pigments are water-clean-up. Do not eat or drink at the marbling tray. Metallic particles cling to the tray interior: rinse thoroughly after a metallic session.

items.name: Marbling acrylic, liquid, in 3-4 base colours
Mica powder, gold or silver, fine ground
items.substitutions: Bronze, copper, or pearl mica powders all work. Coarse-ground mica does not suspend as well and produces less even coverage.

items.name: Eyedroppers, one per colour plus one for the metallic mix

content.text: Measure a small amount of already-extended acrylic pigment (any light colour; white or pale yellow works well as a carrier) into a small container. Add mica powder gradually, stirring, until the mixture has a metallic sheen throughout and no dry clumps remain. The powder must be fully suspended in the pigment before it goes in the dropper: dry clumps sink to the bottom of the tray and do not float.

Metallic pigments have higher surface tension than standard acrylic colours. Add ox-gall to the metallic mix, one drop at a time, until a test drop on the size surface spreads to a circle of similar diameter to the base colours. You will typically need slightly more ox-gall in the metallic mix than in the base colours.

Lay the base colours on the size surface in the pattern you want. For a stone pattern, drop them randomly across the tray. For a Spanish wave, lay them in stripes and comb once at an angle before adding the metallic drops.

Add the metallic colour last. Drop it lightly on top of the base colours already on the size, not in blank areas of size. Where the metallic drop lands on another colour, the high surface tension causes it to push that colour slightly outward and sit on top as a bright metallic spot. The result across the pattern is a series of metallic dots with radiating veins of the carrier colour.

For a vein effect rather than spots, use a stylus to draw the metallic colour lightly through the base colours in one or two passes after dropping. This stretches the metallic drops into thin veins. Do not over-work: two or three stylus strokes are enough.

Lower the mordanted sheet onto the surface, leave for five to ten seconds, and peel up from one edge. Rinse gently under slow-running cold water. The metallic particles cling to the mordanted surface and transfer cleanly.

After a metallic session, mica particles cling to the sides and base of the tray more stubbornly than standard pigments. Empty the size into a waste container, then rinse the tray with clean water and wipe the interior with a soft cloth. Mica in the drain is not hazardous at home quantities but accumulates over time: empty the waste size outside or into a compost bin rather than down the kitchen sink repeatedly.

items.problem: Metallic mix sinks to the bottom of the tray
items.cause: Mica powder not fully mixed into the pigment, or insufficient ox-gall in the metallic mix
items.fix: Stir the metallic mix thoroughly before each dropper-load; mica settles quickly in liquid. Add more ox-gall until the surface tension is low enough for the mix to float.

items.problem: Metallic colour looks dull or muddy on the finished sheet
items.cause: Too much carrier pigment relative to mica powder, or mica powder too coarse
items.fix: Increase the mica-to-pigment ratio. Use fine-ground mica rather than coarse. The metallic sheen is strongest when the mica particle density in the mix is high.

items.problem: Metallic drop does not comb cleanly
items.cause: Metallic pigments have higher surface tension and respond differently to the comb than standard colours
items.fix: Avoid using metallic colour on precise combed patterns. Restrict the metallic to accent drops on stone or wave patterns; use the stylus rather than the comb for any patterning in the metallic area.

content.text: Metallic-accented marbling works well as decorative endpaper for case-bound books, as a journal wrap, and as an envelope liner. The metallic catches light on folded covers and reads well against a dark base colour. Try different mica colours: copper on deep blue, silver on black, bronze on terracotta. These are all standard combinations in the traditional marbled-paper palette.
